result:
Here is what comes, in exactly 1,000 words.

***

The light at this hour is a liar. It slants through the dust motes in my garage workshop, gilding the shavings on the floor, making the rust on the old vise look like antique bronze. It promises clarity, a final understanding, but it only illuminates the mess. I am looking for a specific drill bit—a 3mm brad point, I’m sure of it—but my hands sift through the jars and trays with a mind of their own. They find instead a hexagonal nut, cold and precise; a fossilized rubber band; a pencil stub with teeth marks that are not mine.

This is how it begins. The search for one thing becomes an archaeology of the self. Each object is a strata. Here, in the third drawer, beneath the tangled Christmas lights (why are they here?), I find the ticket stub. It’s for a film I saw alone in a city I no longer live in, on a Tuesday night in November, fifteen years ago. The paper is soft at the edges, the ink fading. I remember the film was mediocre, but the act of going alone felt like a secret victory, a declaration of independence from a loneliness I had just begun to name. The feeling returns, not as nostalgia, but as a physical echo in the hollow under my ribs. I put the stub in my pocket. The drill bit is forgotten.

The light is shifting. The gilded dust turns to ordinary floating grime. I move to the bookshelf, not for a book, but because a sliver of spine catches my eye. It’s a field guide to birds of North America, swollen with damp, its pages wavy. My father’s. He was not a birder. He bought it on a whim at a garage sale, thinking it might be a good thing to know. He’d stand at the kitchen window, squinting at the feeder, this book open to “Finches,” his finger tracing the illustrations. “I think it’s a purple one,” he’d say, though it was always a house sparrow. He never learned the calls. The book is a monument to an intention that never solidified, a path not taken. I flip it open and a pressed maple key, brown and fragile, drifts down. From a tree that’s surely gone now.

This is the true inventory. Not of tools, but of these frozen gestures. We are all curators of abandoned futures. The garage is my museum. There is the yoga mat, rolled tight and webbed with spiders, from the phase of believing flexibility would solve everything. The bag of mortar mix, three pounds left, from the weekend I decided to build a stone wall and built only a sore back. The perfectly good luggage set, bought for a world of travel that was replaced, abruptly, by a world of responsibilities that fit in a backpack.

I step outside to escape the weight of it. The evening air is cool. The lying light is now a thin band of apricot on the horizon, and the first star—or is it a satellite?—punctures the deepening blue. A neighbor starts a lawnmower, the sound violent and domestic. The smell of cut grass is the smell of my entire childhood, a Proustian trigger so universal it’s almost a cliché. Yet it works. I am ten, and the world is just this yard, this street, this infinite summer evening where the only urgency is to catch fireflies before the grown-ups call you in.

We spend our lives building structures against time. Careers, families, houses, reputations. We pour the concrete of habit, raise the walls of routine, nail on the siding of identity. And we believe in it. But then you come into the garage, into the attic, into the quiet corners of your own mind, and you find the evidence that it’s all makeshift. That you are, at your core, a magpie, a collector of moments that shone for a second before being buried by the next.

I think of my friend who, after her mother died, had to empty the house. She said the hardest part wasn’t the jewelry or the photos, but the half-used tubes of hand cream, the particular brand of tea bags, the grocery lists still on the fridge. The archaeology of a life is in the mundane, the unfinished, the things that presumed a tomorrow. My drill bit, wherever it is, presumes a tomorrow where I will need a clean 3mm hole. That is an act of wild optimism.

The satellite (I’ve decided it is one) tracks steadily across the sky. A human thing in the inhuman dark. We launch them to connect us, to watch the weather, to spy, to broadcast. We are desperate to make marks, to be seen, to extend our reach beyond this fragile atmosphere. It is the same impulse that makes me save a ticket stub. *I was here. I saw this. It mattered.*

A moth batters itself against the porch light. Its shadow is frantic and huge on the wall. I wonder what compels it. Is the light so beautiful it is worth dying for? Or is it just a mistake, a fatal bug in its code, mistaking tungsten for the moon? We are like that, too. Drawn to bright, burning lies—of permanence, of meaning, of legacy—and we beat ourselves to pieces against them.

But maybe not. Maybe the moth is not confused. Maybe in that frantic, short dance toward the light, it feels more alive than it ever did hiding in the darkness of the leaves. Maybe the burning is the point.

I go back inside, to the garage. I don’t turn on the overhead fluorescents. I use the small, warm task lamp on my bench. In its pool of yellow light, the world simplifies. My hands know what to do. They bypass the jars, go to the old magnetic strip on the wall, hidden behind a calendar from 2019. And there it is. The 3mm brad point bit, right where it should be, between the 2.5 and the exposed pile of thin universe, a universe where I am both the archaeologist and the artifact, the curator and the collection. I pick up a piece of scrap pine, clamp it in the vise. I bring the drill down. The bit bites in with a clean, whispering sound, sending up a tiny plume of scented dust. The hole is perfect, edges sharp, depth exact. It goes nowhere. It holds nothing. It is, for now, just a perfect absence.

But the act of making it feels like a prayer. Not to a god, but to the process itself. To the attention required. To the hand and the eye and the tool working as one. In this focused making, the ghosts of the ticket stub and the bird book and the mortar mix recede. They are not gone. They are in the walls of this room, the patina on my hands. But for this moment, I am not sifting through the past. I am meeting the present on its own terms, creating a small, new, useless fact in the world.

The night is fully here now. The garage is a cave of shadows beyond my lamp. I will put the drill bit back on its strip. I will, perhaps, finally throw away the petrified rubber band. Or maybe I won’t. Maybe I’ll leave it for a future self to find, on some other evening when the light is lying again, and the search for one thing becomes a conversation with everything I’ve ever been, and everything I’ve ever left behind. The hole in the wood will still be here, too. A tiny, dark well in the grain. Not a mark of something missing, but a witness that on this night, I was here, and I made this.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
